old_claim.note: Logistics & Supply Lines provides a capability that enables this technology without being the only possible path.

new_claim.note: Growing logistical support and formation size increased command-and-control demands addressed by professional officer education, but logistics remained curriculum knowledge rather than a capability required to create a staff college.

source_shape.source_locator: Journal pp. 124-125, Phase One and Phase Two: increased logistical support and formation size created command-and-control challenges, while staff colleges trained higher-level experts to integrate combined arms.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: Increasing logistical support, larger formations, and combined-arms integration created professional knowledge demands addressed by military education and staff colleges.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: This supports logistics as part of the problem domain officers studied, but it does not show that supply systems technically created or universally enabled the college as an institution. A contextual edge preserves that distinction.

ontology_before: military_staff_colleges treated logistics systems as a generic enabling capability without evidence distinguishing institutional causation from curriculum content.
ontology_after: military_staff_colleges retains logistics only as shared professional knowledge associated with complex command, not as a capability required to establish the school.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed and locked by graph invariant 2026-07-16-military-staff-college-scope: logistics_supply_lines remains directly related but only as a common dependency.
would_reject_if: A reliable comparative history demonstrates that a logistics system is a constitutive institutional requirement for every staff college in the scoped tier.
The military_staff_colleges node is narrowed to a specialist logistics school rather than general command and staff education.
short_reason: Logistics was relevant staff knowledge and a source of command complexity, but not the enabling mechanism that created staff colleges.
